subject: Physics
q:
A particle of mass m is moving along the side of a square of side 'a', with a uniform speed υ in the x-y plane as shown in the figure:


Which of the following statements is false for the angular momentum L→ about the origin?
question:
A particle of mass m is moving along the side of a square of side 'a', with a uniform speed υ in the x-y plane as shown in the figure:


Which of the following statements is false for the angular momentum L→ about the origin?
options:
L→=mυR2+ak^

when the particle is moving from B to C.

L→=mυ2Rk^

when the particle is moving from D to A.

L→=-mυ2Rk^

when the particle is moving from A to B.

L→=mυR2+ak^

when the particle is moving from C to D.
solution:
L→=r→×P→ or L→=rpsin⁡θn^

For D to A, L→=R2mv-k^

For A to B, L→=R2mv-k^

For C to D, L→=R2+amvk^

For B to C, L→=R2+amvk^

subject: Physics
q: The position vector of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> object is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>r</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and its velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The magnitude of its angular momentum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: The position vector of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> object is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>r</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and its velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The magnitude of its angular momentum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
Angular momentum of a particle is given by,

L→=r→×P→=r→×mv→ also m=1 kg.

⇒L→=3i^-j^×3j^+k^=9k^-3j^-i^ N m s

The magnitude of the angular momentum will be,

⇒L→=92+32+12=91 N m s

Therefore, the value of x=91.

q: A ball of mass 10 kg moving with a velocity 103 m s-1 along X-axis, hits another ball of mass 20 kg which is at rest. After the collision, the first ball comes to rest and the second one disintegrates into two equal pieces. One of the pieces starts moving along Y-axis at a speed of 10 m s-1. The second piece starts moving at a speed of 20 m s-1 at an angle θ (degree) with respect to the X-axis. The configuration of pieces after the collision is shown in the figure. The value of $\theta$ to the nearest integer is _________.
question: A ball of mass 10 kg moving with a velocity 103 m s-1 along X-axis, hits another ball of mass 20 kg which is at rest. After the collision, the first ball comes to rest and the second one disintegrates into two equal pieces. One of the pieces starts moving along Y-axis at a speed of 10 m s-1. The second piece starts moving at a speed of 20 m s-1 at an angle θ (degree) with respect to the X-axis. The configuration of pieces after the collision is shown in the figure. The value of $\theta$ to the nearest integer is _________.
solution:
Before Collision 

After Collision 

From conservation of momentum along x axis;

P→i=P→f

10×103=200cosθ

cosθ=32

θ=30°

subject: Physics
q: An equilateral triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$ is cut from a thin solid sheet of wood. (See figure) $\mathrm{D}, \mathrm{E}$ and $\mathrm{F}$ are the mid-points of its sides as shown and $\mathrm{G}$ is the centre of the triangle. The moment of inertia of the triangle about an axis passing through $\mathrm{G}$ and perpendicular to the plane of the triangle is $\mathrm{I}_{0}$. If the smaller triangle $\mathrm{DEF}$ is removed from $\mathrm{ABC}$, the moment of inertia of the remaining figure about the same axis is $I$. Then
question: An equilateral triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$ is cut from a thin solid sheet of wood. (See figure) $\mathrm{D}, \mathrm{E}$ and $\mathrm{F}$ are the mid-points of its sides as shown and $\mathrm{G}$ is the centre of the triangle. The moment of inertia of the triangle about an axis passing through $\mathrm{G}$ and perpendicular to the plane of the triangle is $\mathrm{I}_{0}$. If the smaller triangle $\mathrm{DEF}$ is removed from $\mathrm{ABC}$, the moment of inertia of the remaining figure about the same axis is $I$. Then
solution:
Let mass of the larger triangle $=\mathrm{M}$ Side of larger triangle $=\ell$ Moment of inertia of larger triangle $=\mathrm{ma}^{2}$

Mass of smaller triangle $=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{4}$

Length of smaller triangle $=\frac{\ell}{2}$

Moment of inertia of removed triangle $=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{4}\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}\right)^{2}$

$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{I}_{\text {removed }}}{\mathrm{I}_{\text {original }}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{M}}{4}}{\mathrm{M}} \cdot \frac{\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}\right)^{2}}{(\mathrm{a})^{2}}$

$I_{\text {removed }}=\frac{I_{0}}{16}$

$\mathrm{So}, \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{0}-\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{16}=\frac{15 \mathrm{I}_{0}}{16}$

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is rotated about one of its diameter with angular speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the moment of inertia of the sphere about its tangent is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> times its angular momentum about the diameter. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
question: A solid sphere of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is rotated about one of its diameter with angular speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the moment of inertia of the sphere about its tangent is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> times its angular momentum about the diameter. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
solution:
The angular momentum about the diameter is 

Ldiameter =25MR2ω;

The moment of inertia of a sphere is ICM=25MR2. The moment of inertia about the tangent is 

Itangent =ICM+MR2=25MR2+MR2=75MR2.

It is given that ω=10 rad s-1

Using the given relation between the moment of inertia and the angular momentum,

75MR2=x×10-2×25MR2ω⇒x=3.5×102ω=3.5×10=35

q: A mass m is supported by a massless string wound around a uniform hollow cylinder of mass m and radius R. If the string does not slip on the cylinder, with what acceleration will the mass fall on release?
question: A mass m is supported by a massless string wound around a uniform hollow cylinder of mass m and radius R. If the string does not slip on the cylinder, with what acceleration will the mass fall on release?
solution:
mg - T = ma      ----- 1

TR = Iα

TR =mR 2 α

​∴T=mRα   ----2

For no slippping

a = Rα  ---3

putting (3) in (2) 

T = ma ---4

Adding 1 and 4

mg = 2ma

∴   a = g 2

When the circle is rotating with angular speed ω about the vertical diameter AB, as shown in figure, the bead is at rest with respect to the circular ring at position P as shown. Then the value of ω2 is equal to:
question: A smooth wire of length 2πr is bent into a circle and kept in a vertical plane. A bead can slide smoothly on the wire. When the circle is rotating with angular speed ω about the vertical diameter AB, as shown in figure, the bead is at rest with respect to the circular ring at position P as shown. Then the value of ω2 is equal to:
solution:
From free body diagram of bead,
Ncos30°=mg .... (1)
Nsin30°=mr2ω2 ..... (2)
By dividing equation (2) by equation (1)
tan30° = rω22g
ω2 = 2gr3

q: A rod of length 50 cm is pivoted at one end. It is raised such that it makes an angle of 30o from the horizontal as shown and released from rest. Its angular speed when it passes through the horizontal (in rad s-1 ) will be g=10 ms-2

solution:
From conservation of mechanical energy


Loss in gravitational PE= gain in KE

mgl2sin⁡30o=12Iω2

mgl2sin⁡30o=12ml23ω2

ω=3g2l=3×102×50×10-2=30 rad/s

They are brought in contact with each other and thereafter they rotate with a common angular velocity. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>f</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></math> are the final and initial total energies, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">i</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
question: Two coaxial discs, having moments of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> , are rotating with respective angular velocities <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><mi> </mi><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> , about their common axis. They are brought in contact with each other and thereafter they rotate with a common angular velocity. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>f</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></math> are the final and initial total energies, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">i</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
As external torque is zero, angular momentum remains conserved.
I1ω1+I12ω12=I1+I12ω
∴ Common angular velocity ω=5ω16
∴ Ef-Ei=12×I1+I12ω2-12×I1ω1 2-12×Il2ω122
=12×3I1256ω12-916I1ω12
=75 I1ω12-81 I1ω12144
=-6I1ω12144
=-I1ω1224

q: ABC is a plane lamina of the shape of an equilateral triangle. D, E are mid-points of AB, AC and G is the centroid of the lamina. Moment of inertia of the lamina about an axis passing through G and perpendicular to the plane ABC is I0. If part ADE is removed, the moment of inertia of the remaining part about the same axis is NI016 where N is an integer. Value of N is:
question: ABC is a plane lamina of the shape of an equilateral triangle. D, E are mid-points of AB, AC and G is the centroid of the lamina. Moment of inertia of the lamina about an axis passing through G and perpendicular to the plane ABC is I0. If part ADE is removed, the moment of inertia of the remaining part about the same axis is NI016 where N is an integer. Value of N is:
solution:
Let mass of lamina =m, and length of side =l, then moment of inertila of lamina about an axis passing through G and perpendicular to the plane.

I0∝ml2

I0=kml2

Let moment of inertila of DEF=I1 about G

then, I1=I016

then,IADE=IBDF=IEFC=I2

3I2+I1=I0

3I2+I016=I0

⇒ I2=5I016

So, Moment of inertila of DECB=2I2+I125I016+I016

=11I016

question: A rigid massless rod of length 3l has two masses attached at each end as shown in the figure. The rod is pivoted at point P on the horizontal axis. When released from the initial horizontal position, its instantaneous angular acceleration will be

 τnet=Iα (where I→moment if inertia about axis of rotation)

5mgl-2mg2l=2m2l2+5ml2α

α=g13l

q: An L -shaped object, made of thin rods of uniform mass density, is suspended with a string as shown in figure. If AB=BC, and the angle made by AB with downward vertical is θ, then:
question: An L -shaped object, made of thin rods of uniform mass density, is suspended with a string as shown in figure. If AB=BC, and the angle made by AB with downward vertical is θ, then:
solution:
At equilibrium, the centre of mass will be below the point of suspension.

Taking torque about point O,

mgl2sin⁡θ+mglsin⁡θ-l2cos⁡θ=0

⇒  3l2sin⁡θ=l2cos⁡θ

⇒ tan⁡θ=13

⇒ θ=tan-113

q: A ring and a solid sphere rotating about an axis passing through their centres have same radii of gyration. The axis of rotation is perpendicular to plane of ring. The ratio of radius of ring to that of sphere is 2x. The value of x is _____.
question: A ring and a solid sphere rotating about an axis passing through their centres have same radii of gyration. The axis of rotation is perpendicular to plane of ring. The ratio of radius of ring to that of sphere is 2x. The value of x is _____.
solution:
The moment of inertia of the ring about an axis perpendicular to the plane of ring is Ir=mk2.

The moment of inertia of the sphere is 

Is=25mR2s

It is given that 

mk2=mR2r=25mRs2   ...(i)

From equation (i)

Rr=25Rs

From the question, it can be written that

RrRs=2x

⇒RrRs=25⇒x=5

q: Moment of inertia of a cylinder of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to the axis of the cylinder is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>M</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>12</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> If such a cylinder is to be made for a given mass of a material, the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></mfrac></math> for it to have minimum possible <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is:
question: Moment of inertia of a cylinder of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to the axis of the cylinder is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>M</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>12</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> If such a cylinder is to be made for a given mass of a material, the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></mfrac></math> for it to have minimum possible <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is:
solution:
Let a cylinder of mass m1, length L and radius R then take elementary disc of radius R and trickiness dx at distance of x from axis OO' then moment of inertia about OO' as this element

dl=dmR24+dmx2

I=∫dl=∫dmR24+∫n=L/2n=-L/2MLdx×x2

⇒I=MR24+ML212

⇒I=M4×VπL+ML212

dIdL=-mV4πL2+M×2L12=0

⇒    V=23πL3    ⇒    πR2L=23πL3    ⇒    LR=32I=MV4πL+ML212

v=ωr

subject: Physics
q: An object of uniform density rolls up the curved path with the initial velocity $v_0$ as shown in the figure. If the maximum height attained by an object is $\dfrac{7v_0^2}{10g}$ ($g$ = acceleration due to gravity), the object is a _______.
question: An object of uniform density rolls up the curved path with the initial velocity $v_0$ as shown in the figure. If the maximum height attained by an object is $\dfrac{7v_0^2}{10g}$ ($g$ = acceleration due to gravity), the object is a _______.
solution:
By the law of conservation of mechanical energy, the total initial kinetic energy of the rolling object is converted into potential energy at the maximum height.

Total initial kinetic energy for an object rolling without slipping is given by:
$K = K_{\text{translational}} + K_{\text{rotational}}$
$K = \dfrac{1}{2}mv_0^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$

Substituting $\omega = \dfrac{v_0}{R}$ and $I = mk^2$ (where $k$ is the radius of gyration):
$K = \dfrac{1}{2}mv_0^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}(mk^2)\left(\dfrac{v_0}{R}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}mv_0^2\left(1 + \dfrac{k^2}{R^2}\right)$

The potential energy at the maximum height $h$ is:
$U = mgh$

Given $h = \dfrac{7v_0^2}{10g}$, we have:
$U = mg\left(\dfrac{7v_0^2}{10g}\right) = \dfrac{7}{10}mv_0^2$

Equating kinetic and potential energy ($K = U$):
$\dfrac{1}{2}mv_0^2\left(1 + \dfrac{k^2}{R^2}\right) = \dfrac{7}{10}mv_0^2$

$1 + \dfrac{k^2}{R^2} = \dfrac{14}{10} = \dfrac{7}{5}$

$\dfrac{k^2}{R^2} = \dfrac{7}{5} - 1 = \dfrac{2}{5}$

The moment of inertia is $I = \dfrac{2}{5}mR^2$, which corresponds to a solid sphere.

Answer: solid sphere

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a uniform speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Y</mi></math> plane along the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. The angular momentum of the particle about the origin will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a uniform speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Y</mi></math> plane along the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. The angular momentum of the particle about the origin will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Equation of line given: y-x-4=0.

Let d1 be the perpendicular distance of given line from origin.

d1=-412+12=22

So, |L→|=mvd1=5×32×22 kg m2 s-1

=60 kg m2 s-1

question: A uniform rod of length ' ℓ' is pivoted at one of its ends on a vertical shaft of negligible radius. When the shaft rotates at angular speed ω the rod makes an angle θwith it (see figure). To find θ equate the rate of change of angular momentum (direction going into the paper) mℓ212ω2sinθ about the centre of mass (CM) to the torque provided by the horizontal and vertical forces FHand Fv about the CM. The value of θ is then such that:
solution:
Torque of centrifugal force τcf=dm.x sin θω2xcos ϑ=mlω2 sin θcos θ∫0lx2dx

τef=ml2ω2sinθ cosθ3

τmg=τcf

mg.l2sin θ=ml2ω2 sinθ cos θ3

cos θ=3g2lω2

subject: Physics
q: A uniform $\operatorname{rod} A B$ is suspended from a point $X$, at a variable distance from $x$ from $A$, as shown. To make the rod horizontal, a mass $m$ is suspended from its end $A$. A set of $(m, x)$ values is recorded. The appropriate variable that give a straight line, when plotted, are:
question: A uniform $\operatorname{rod} A B$ is suspended from a point $X$, at a variable distance from $x$ from $A$, as shown. To make the rod horizontal, a mass $m$ is suspended from its end $A$. A set of $(m, x)$ values is recorded. The appropriate variable that give a straight line, when plotted, are:
solution:
Balancing torque w.r.t. point of suspension $$ \begin{aligned} &m g x=M g\left(\frac{\ell}{2}-x\right) \\ &\Rightarrow m x=M \frac{\ell}{2}-M x \\ &m=\left(M \frac{\ell}{2}\right) \frac{1}{x}-M \end{aligned} $$
$y=\alpha \frac{1}{x}-C \quad$ Straight line equation.

subject: Physics
q: A force of 49 N acts tangentially at the highest point of a sphere (solid) of mass 20 kg , kept on a rough horizontal plane. If the sphere rolls without slipping, then the acceleration of the center of the sphere is
question: A force of 49 N acts tangentially at the highest point of a sphere (solid) of mass 20 kg , kept on a rough horizontal plane. If the sphere rolls without slipping, then the acceleration of the center of the sphere is
solution:
Torque about bottom point
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{F} \times 2 \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{I} \alpha \\ & 49 \times 2 \mathrm{r}=\frac{7}{5} \mathrm{mr}^2 \alpha \\ & 14=4 \mathrm{r} \alpha
\end{aligned}$
As sphere rolls without slipping
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{r} \alpha \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{14}{4}=\frac{7}{2}=3.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The following bodies are made to roll up (without slipping) the same inclined plane from a horizontal plane: (i) a ring of radius <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> (ii) a solid cylinder of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and (iii) a solid sphere of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> If, in each case, the speed of the center of mass at the bottom of the incline is same, the ratio of the maximum heights they climb is:
question: The following bodies are made to roll up (without slipping) the same inclined plane from a horizontal plane: (i) a ring of radius <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> (ii) a solid cylinder of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and (iii) a solid sphere of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> If, in each case, the speed of the center of mass at the bottom of the incline is same, the ratio of the maximum heights they climb is:
solution:
From conservation of mechanical energy.
12mRv2+12IRωr2=mRghR⇒V22+V22=ghR
12mdv2+12Idωd2=mdghd⇒V22+V24=ghd
12msv2+12Isωs2=msghs⇒V22+V25=ghs
∴  hR:hd:hs=1:34:710=20:15:14

subject: Physics
q: Four point masses, each of value $\mathrm{m}$, are placed at the corners of a square $A B C D$ of side $\ell$. The moment of inertia through $A$ and parallel to $B D$ is
question: Four point masses, each of value $\mathrm{m}$, are placed at the corners of a square $A B C D$ of side $\ell$. The moment of inertia through $A$ and parallel to $B D$ is
solution: $\mathrm{I}=2 \mathrm{~m}(\ell / \sqrt{2})^2=3 \mathrm{~m} \ell^2$

subject: Physics
q: Consider a disc of mass 5 kg, radius 2 m, rotating with angular velocity of 10 rad s-1 about an axis perpendicular to the plane of rotation. An identical disc is kept gently over the rotating disc along the same axis. The energy dissipated so that both the discs continue to rotate together without slipping is _________J.
question: Consider a disc of mass 5 kg, radius 2 m, rotating with angular velocity of 10 rad s-1 about an axis perpendicular to the plane of rotation. An identical disc is kept gently over the rotating disc along the same axis. The energy dissipated so that both the discs continue to rotate together without slipping is _________J.
solution:
Initial angular momentum, Li=Iωi=MR22·ω=100 kg m2 s-1

Initial kinetic energy, Ei=12·MR22·ω2=500 J

From conservation of angular momentum, we can write

L→i=L→f⇒100=2Iωf

⇒ωf=5 rad s-1

Ef=2×12·5(2)22·(5)2=250 J

Therefore, required energy loss ΔE=250 J

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is divided into two unequal parts. The smaller part having mass $M/8$ is converted into a sphere of radius $r$ and the larger part is converted into a circular disc of thickness $t$ and radius $2R$. If $I_1$ is moment of inertia of a sphere having radius $r$ about an axis through its centre and $I_2$ is the moment of inertia of a disc about its diameter, the ratio of their moment of inertia $I_2/I_1 = $ _____.
question: A solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is divided into two unequal parts. The smaller part having mass $M/8$ is converted into a sphere of radius $r$ and the larger part is converted into a circular disc of thickness $t$ and radius $2R$. If $I_1$ is moment of inertia of a sphere having radius $r$ about an axis through its centre and $I_2$ is the moment of inertia of a disc about its diameter, the ratio of their moment of inertia $I_2/I_1 = $ _____.
solution:
Let the mass of the original solid sphere be $M$ and its radius be $R$.

Mass of the smaller sphere, $m_1 = \dfrac{M}{8}$.
Since the material remains the same, the density is constant. The volume is directly proportional to the mass.
$\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \dfrac{1}{8} \left(\dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3\right) \Rightarrow r^3 = \dfrac{R^3}{8} \Rightarrow r = \dfrac{R}{2}$

Moment of inertia of the smaller sphere about an axis through its centre is:
$I_1 = \dfrac{2}{5} m_1 r^2 = \dfrac{2}{5} \left(\dfrac{M}{8}\right) \left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{2}{5} \times \dfrac{M}{8} \times \dfrac{R^2}{4} = \dfrac{M R^2}{80}$

Mass of the larger part (which is converted into a disc) is:
$m_2 = M - \dfrac{M}{8} = \dfrac{7M}{8}$

The radius of the disc is given as $2R$. The moment of inertia of a disc about its diameter is:
$I_2 = \dfrac{1}{4} m_2 (\text{radius})^2 = \dfrac{1}{4} \left(\dfrac{7M}{8}\right) (2R)^2 = \dfrac{1}{4} \times \dfrac{7M}{8} \times 4R^2 = \dfrac{7M R^2}{8}$

The ratio of their moments of inertia is:
$\dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = \dfrac{\dfrac{7M R^2}{8}}{\dfrac{M R^2}{80}} = \dfrac{7}{8} \times 80 = 70$

Answer: $70$

subject: Physics
q: Consider a uniform square plate of side ' $a$ ' and mass ' $m$ '. The moment of inertia of this plate about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through one of its corners is
question: Consider a uniform square plate of side ' $a$ ' and mass ' $m$ '. The moment of inertia of this plate about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through one of its corners is
solution:
$$
I=I_{c m}+m\left(\frac{a \sqrt{2}}{2}\right)^2=\frac{m a^2}{6}+\frac{m a^2}{2}=\frac{2}{3} m a^2
$$

q: A thin disc of mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> and radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> has mass per unit area <math><mi mathvariant="normal">σ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> is the distance from its centre. Its moment inertia about an axis going through its centre of mass and perpendicular to its plane is:
question: A thin disc of mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> and radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> has mass per unit area <math><mi mathvariant="normal">σ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> is the distance from its centre. Its moment inertia about an axis going through its centre of mass and perpendicular to its plane is:
solution:
dm=σ⋅dA=σ 2πr dr
=2πk r3 dr
M=∫dm=2πk∫0Rr3dr
=2πkR44=πkR42
dI=dm r2=2πkr5 dr
I=2πk∫0Rr5dr=2πkR66=2πkR63×2

since,  M=πkR42

so,
I=2MR23

subject: Physics
q: Ratio of radius of gyration of a hollow sphere to that of a solid cylinder of equal mass, for moment of Inertia about their diameter axis $\mathrm{AB}$ as shown in figure is $\sqrt{8 / x}$. The value of $x$ is :
question: Ratio of radius of gyration of a hollow sphere to that of a solid cylinder of equal mass, for moment of Inertia about their diameter axis $\mathrm{AB}$ as shown in figure is $\sqrt{8 / x}$. The value of $x$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_{\text {sphere }}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{MR}^2=\mathrm{Mk}_1^2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\text {cylinder }}=\frac{1}{12} \mathrm{M}\left(4 \mathrm{R}^2\right)+\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{MR}^2+\mathrm{M}(2 \mathrm{R})^2 \\ & =\frac{67}{12} \mathrm{MR}^2=\mathrm{Mk}_2^2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{k}_1}{\mathrm{k}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{12}{67}}=\sqrt{\frac{8}{67}}\end{aligned}$

question: Two masses m and m2 are connected at the two ends of a massless rigid rod of length l. The rod is suspended by a thin wire of torsional constant k at the centre of mass of the rod-mass system (see figure). solution:
When rod is rotated by angle α, the twist in the wire is ϕ.

angular frequency of wire, Ω=kI

angular frequency of rod, ω=θ0.Ω

Max. Tension in the rod =mω2.l3

=m θ0 2.kI.l3 , here I=ml23

=θ0 2kl

q: A bob of mass m attached to an inextensible string of length <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>l</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math> is suspended from a vertical support. The bob rotates in a horizontal circle with an angular speed <math> <mrow> <mi>ω</mi> </mrow> </math> rad/s about the vertical. About the point of suspension :
question: A bob of mass m attached to an inextensible string of length <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>l</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math> is suspended from a vertical support. Angular momentum changes both in direction and magnitude

as τ is perpendicular to L → , direction of L changes but magnitude remains same.

q: A metal coin of mass 5 g and radius 1 cm is fixed to a thin stick AB of negligible mass as shown in the figure. The system is initially at rest. The constant torque, that will make the system rotate about AB at 25 rotations per second in  5 s , is close to:
question: A metal coin of mass 5 g and radius 1 cm is fixed to a thin stick AB of negligible mass as shown in the figure. The system is initially at rest. The constant torque, that will make the system rotate about AB at 25 rotations per second in  5 s , is close to:
solution:
As torque τ is constant.
ω=τIt
τ=ωIt=2πn×54mr2t
= 2π×25×54×5×10-3×1×10-225
=196×10-7
≈2⋅0×10-5 N m

subject: Physics
q: A particle performing uniform circular motion has angular frequency is doubled \& its kinetic energy halved, then the new angular momentum is
question: A particle performing uniform circular motion has angular frequency is doubled \& its kinetic energy halved, then the new angular momentum is
solution:
Angular momentum $\propto \frac{1}{\text { Angular frequency }} \propto$ Kinetic energy $\Rightarrow \vec{L}=\frac{\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E} .}{\mathrm{w}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{L}_1}{\mathrm{~L}_2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}_1}{\mathrm{w}_1}\right) \times \frac{\mathrm{w}_2}{\mathrm{KE}_2}=4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}_2=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{4}$

subject: Physics
q: A uniform rod of length <math><mi>l</mi><mi> </mi></math> is being rotated in a horizontal plane with a constant angular speed about an axis passing through one of its ends. If the tension generated in the rod due to rotation is <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> at a distance <math><mi>x</mi></math> from the axis, then which of the following graphs depicts it most closely?
question: A uniform rod of length <math><mi>l</mi><mi> </mi></math> is being rotated in a horizontal plane with a constant angular speed about an axis passing through one of its ends. If the tension generated in the rod due to rotation is <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> at a distance <math><mi>x</mi></math> from the axis, then which of the following graphs depicts it most closely?
solution:
The mass of the rod at a distance x from the axis is dm=ML.dx
T=(dm)ω2x

Integrating the above equation from x to L
=∫xLMLω2x dx
=MLω2x22xL
M2Lω2L2-x2
The above equation depicts the relation between tension and length of rod as downward opening parabola.

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere of mass 5 kg and radius 10 cm is kept in contact with another solid sphere of mass 10 kg and radius 20 cm. The moment of inertia of this pair of spheres about the tangent passing through the point of contact is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{kg}. \mathrm{m}^{2}$.
question: A solid sphere of mass 5 kg and radius 10 cm is kept in contact with another solid sphere of mass 10 kg and radius 20 cm. The moment of inertia of this pair of spheres about the tangent passing through the point of contact is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{kg}. \mathrm{m}^{2}$.
solution:
For two solid spheres in contact with the tangent line at the contact point.
Sphere 1: $m_1 = 5$ kg, $r_1 = 0.1$ m
Sphere 2: $m_2 = 10$ kg, $r_2 = 0.2$ m
For a solid sphere, $I_{cm} = \frac{2}{5}mr^2$
The tangent at contact is perpendicular to the line joining centers. The perpendicular distance from each sphere's center to this tangent line equals its radius.
Using parallel axis theorem: $I_{total} = I_{cm} + md^2$
For sphere 1: $I_1 = \frac{2}{5}(5)(0.1)^2 + 5(0.1)^2 = 0.02 + 0.05 = 0.07$ kg·m²
For sphere 2: $I_2 = \frac{2}{5}(10)(0.2)^2 + 10(0.2)^2 = 0.16 + 0.4 = 0.56$ kg·m²
Total: $I = 0.07 + 0.56 = 0.63$ kg·m²
